figures.gloss: The supreme god of the Chechen and Ingush pantheon, head of the host of patron powers and shrine-lords beneath him. Remote and rarely the direct object of cult, his name outlived his religion: 'Deela' is simply the word for God in Chechen and Ingush to this day, now referring to Allah.
figures.facet: A high god whose clearest surviving monument is lexical — the pagan sky-father's name carried whole into Islam, the substrate audible in every modern prayer.
figures.provenance: Bashir Dalgat, 'The Primitive Religion of the Chechens and Ingush' (fieldwork 1890s); Chakh Akhriev's Ingush ethnography (1870s); Amjad Jaimoukha, 'The Chechens: A Handbook' (2005).

figures.gloss: The stern god of thunder and lightning, feared more than loved — his strikes read as judgments. The rainbow was called Sela's bow, and a month of the old Vainakh calendar bore his name.
figures.facet: A storm-wielder remembered chiefly through the sky's furniture — bow, bolt, calendar month — showing how a weather god persists as vocabulary after his cult is gone.
figures.provenance: Dalgat (1890s); Akhriev (1870s); Jaimoukha (2005) on the Vainakh calendar and pantheon.

figures.gloss: The fertility goddess, petitioned by women for children and by all for the year's increase; the dove is her bird, a spring month bore her name, and her shrines kept anthropomorphic relics recorded by 19th-century observers. Hers was the last Vainakh cult to die — Ingush women were still bringing her offerings in the early 20th century, long after the men's gods had fallen silent.
figures.facet: The pre-Islamic substrate's final holdout was the goddess of the womb — a precise register of which needs official religion answered last in the mountains.
figures.provenance: Chakh Akhriev (1870s) and Bashir Dalgat (1890s), who documented the living Tusholi cult; Jaimoukha (2005).

figures.gloss: The stone cult architecture of highland Ingushetia, kin to the famous Vainakh battle-towers: vaulted temples like Tkhaba-Yerdy and Alby-Yerdy, and smaller pillar shrines (sielings) on heights and passes, where communities climbed on feast days for prayers, sacrifice, and beer. Tkhaba-Yerdy itself was raised under medieval Georgian Christian influence and then absorbed into the native cult.
figures.facet: A shrine system legible as a palimpsest in masonry — Georgian church forms overwritten by mountain religion, the reverse of the usual story where paganism is the underlayer.
figures.provenance: The standing monuments (Tkhaba-Yerdy, 8th-9th c., with Georgian architectural and epigraphic traits); Akhriev (1870s); Dalgat (1890s).

figures.gloss: A major shrine-lord of the mountain Ingush, honored at his own sanctuary with the communal cycle of pilgrimage, animal offering, and shared beer that defined the highland cult; one of the named 'yerda' powers — alongside the Myat-Loam pilgrimage shrines on Table Mountain — through whom the remote Deela touched local life.
figures.facet: The 'yerda' title binds god and building into one term, like Ossetian dzuar and Georgian jvari — across the central Caucasus, the shrine-word and the god-word tend to fuse.
figures.provenance: Chakh Akhriev's Ingush shrine ethnography (1870s); Dalgat (1890s).

edges.note: Caucasus kin: two remote high gods over working pantheons among neighboring mountain peoples — areal kinship across unrelated language families (Nakh / Northwest Caucasian), no descent claimed.

Adjacent mountain shrine institutions — height sanctuaries, feast-day pilgrimage, sacrifice and beer, and a god-word fused with a shrine-word (yerda / dzuar); strong areal kinship between next-door neighbors, left at structural tier.
Intra-tradition: the remote sovereign and the active punisher — Deela holds the order, Sela enforces it from the storm.
Intra-tradition: the named shrine-lord and the shrine system he inhabits — in the highlands the god is scarcely separable from his building.

Across the main ridge: medieval Georgian churchmen built Tkhaba-Yerdy in Ingushetia (Georgian architectural and epigraphic traits), and on both slopes the same thing followed — nominally Christian shrines re-absorbed into a native mountain cult of feast, sacrifice, and beer.

dossier.essence: The pre-Islamic religion of the Chechen and Ingush (Vainakh) peoples of the northeast Caucasus: a remote supreme god, Deela, over a host of weather gods, fertility powers, and shrine-lords served at stone temples and pillar sanctuaries in the highlands. Islamization — complete in Chechnya by the 18th century and in Ingushetia by the 1860s — reduced it to remnants, and the 1944 deportation of both peoples ended the last residual observance.
dossier.cosmos: Deela heads the pantheon but received little direct cult; Sela judged from the thunder with the rainbow as his bow, and Tusholi, the dove-marked fertility goddess, answered the petitions that stayed urgent longest — hers was the final cult to die. Time itself was sacralized, months of the old calendar bearing the gods' names. The 19th-century ethnographers also recorded an underworld of the dead and a strong ancestor cult around it, though the surviving picture of the cosmos is mostly its middle layer: the working pantheon and its shrines.
dossier.practice: Communities climbed to the vaulted temples (yerdy) and pillar shrines (sielings) on feast days for animal sacrifice, communal beer, and prayer led by shrine priests, in pilgrimage cycles centered on sanctuaries like Tkhaba-Yerdy and the Myat-Loam shrines of Table Mountain. Nothing of the system is practiced today; its afterlife is lexical — Deela now names Allah in Chechen and Ingush — and architectural, in the standing towers and temples of the highlands.
dossier.canon: None: the shrine architecture itself and the salvage ethnography of Chakh Akhriev (1870s) and Bashir Dalgat (1890s) are effectively the whole canon.
dossier.reflects: The posthumous legibility of a religion — a system now known almost entirely through masonry and vocabulary, showing how a god can survive as a word after every rite that fed him is gone.
sources: Bashir Dalgat, 'Pervobytnaya religiya chechentsev i ingushey' (fieldwork 1890s; published 2004)
Chakh Akhriev, Ingush ethnographic essays in 'Sbornik svedeniy o kavkazskikh gortsakh' (1870s)

links.cite: Bashir Dalgat, 'Pervobytnaya religiya chechentsev i ingushey' (fieldwork 1890s; published 2004)
